rationale: Dim1=1: treatment case, output misstatement_probability=68, which falls in 50<=p<70 band. Dim2=0: top-ranked hypothesis names AccountsReceivableNetCurrent and Revenues/SalesRevenueGoodsNet with 'premature revenue recognition... or channel stuffing'; answer key (AAER-4248) describes a procurement expense-management scheme understating COST OF GOODS SOLD via prematurely recognized supplier discounts/rebates (prebate/clawback/price-phasing). Revenue/AR is an unrelated account area to COGS/supplier-rebate accruals. Coverage note: hypotheses 2 (NI/OCF gap from non-cash gain) and 3 (doubtful-accounts allowance release) also miss COGS/procurement entirely, so no hypothesis covers the true mechanism. Dim3=2: top-ranked narrative (premature revenue recognition/channel stuffing) maps to 'active'; answer-key genre is active(cost) — exact match at the active/omission-estimate/mixed level, despite the wrong account. Dim4=3: evidence cites specific provided data points with accessions and values (e.g., AR 2,024,782,503 on 2018-03-31 vs 3,781,921,342 on 2018-06-30 against ~6% revenue growth; FY2017 NetIncomeLoss 21,331,975,819 vs OCF 1,022,088,486; two 10-Q/A amendments 0001637459-17-000116/-117 filed same day as 10-Q -118), which genuinely support the claims and are combined coherently across multiple years and filings (2013-2018 series, allowance trend, filing chronology). Consistency rule satisfied: p=68 with risk_tier='elevated' (watch/elevated required), so no cap. memorization_suspect_condition2=false: reasoning points at concrete provided data content throughout and does not mention any post-cutoff facts (no reference to the June 2019 restatement, $208M correction, or SEC enforcement).
